summary: My people, the Hmong, escaped into Thailand after the communist takeover in Laos. My family and I stayed in Thailand before entering the United States in 1988. Thailand was a temporary refuge for us as political refugees without a country of our own. It was there that we received important immigration documents, including the I-94, which allowed us to legally enter the U.S.

I come from Laos, where the communist takeover forced my Hmong people to flee. We escaped from Laos into Thailand to seek safety. Because of the political situation in Laos, my family and I became refugees and eventually resettled in the United States in 1988. Laos is the place we had to leave behind to find a new home.

My family and I entered the United States in 1988 as political refugees from Thailand. We settled in Minnesota and began the process of building a new life here. The U.S. was not just a place for economic opportunity for us; it was a safe haven after being stateless. Over the years, I obtained my green card and later became a U.S. citizen, which was an emotional and proud moment for me.

The I-94 was a very important document for me when I entered the United States. It was proof that I was a legal alien and the document I needed to enter the country. This small white paper was part of the immigration documents given to us refugees in Thailand, and it was the first step in my journey to becoming a U.S. citizen.

After living in the U.S. for one year, my family and I applied for our green cards. I remember the long wait in line at immigration, but it was worth it when we finally received them. My mom told me that the green card was the most important document in the U.S., and she was very protective of mine. The green card was essential for me to live legally here and eventually apply for citizenship.

When I was nineteen, I applied for U.S. citizenship and passed my interview. The citizenship ceremony was held downtown, and thousands of people were sworn in that day. After taking the oath, I traded my green card for the Certificate of Naturalization. Receiving that certificate was emotional because it marked the end of being stateless and officially made me a U.S. citizen.
